title: Key AI Developments This Week: Frontier Models, Robotics, and Real-Time Editing Tools
summary:
This week in AI has seen groundbreaking advancements across multiple domains, from frontier models trained without NVIDIA GPUs to real-time video editing and robotics. Chinese food delivery company Meituan released LongCat 2.0, a 1.6 trillion parameter model trained entirely on alternative AI ASIC superpods, demonstrating the viability of non-NVIDIA hardware for large-scale AI training. Other notable releases include Muse VIT for sheet music understanding, LiveEdit for real-time video editing, and Meta’s Brain to QWERTY, which converts brain signals into text without surgery.

The robotics space also saw significant progress with tools like Omni-Contact for humanoid robots and VidiHand for reconstructing 3D hand movements from videos. Additionally, Anthropic’s re-release of Claude 3.5 Fable came with stricter safety measures, impacting its usability for coding tasks. Google introduced lightweight and efficient models like Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash, while innovations like RDM and Mr. Flow offered new ways to speed up image generation by up to 20 times.
key_takeaways: Meituan’s LongCat 2.0 is a 1.6 trillion parameter model trained without NVIDIA GPUs, using alternative AI ASIC superpods.
Muse VIT is a specialized AI model for understanding sheet music, trained on 9.7 million pages of sheet music.
LiveEdit enables real-time video editing at 13 frames per second, supporting tasks like object replacement and style changes.
Meta’s Brain to QWERTY converts brain signals into text with 69% character accuracy and 78% word accuracy, without requiring surgery.
Anthropic’s re-released Claude 3.5 Fable is more restricted, with coding tasks often falling back to the less capable Opus 4.8 model.
Google’s Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash prioritize speed and cost-efficiency for image and video generation.
RDM and Mr. Flow offer methods to speed up image generation by up to 20 times, with minimal quality loss.

sections.heading: Muse VIT: AI for Sheet Music Understanding
sections.body:
Muse VIT is a specialized AI model designed to read and understand sheet music. Unlike general vision models, Muse VIT is trained to interpret the complex relationships between symbols, timing, pitch, staff lines, and structure in sheet music. The model was trained on 9.7 million pages of sheet music, covering approximately 400,000 musical works. During training, parts of the sheet music were masked, and the AI was tasked with reconstructing the missing sections, enabling it to learn the underlying structure and notation of sheet music.

Compared to other vision models, Muse VIT demonstrates superior performance in recognizing and classifying sheet music. The model is relatively small, under 500 MB, making it suitable for running on most consumer devices. The project is open-source, with instructions available for local execution.

sections.heading: LongCat 2.0: A Frontier Model Without NVIDIA GPUs
sections.body:
Meituan, a Chinese food delivery company, released LongCat 2.0, a 1.6 trillion parameter frontier model trained entirely without NVIDIA GPUs. This is a significant achievement, as NVIDIA GPUs are typically considered the gold standard for AI training. Instead, Meituan used AI ASIC superpods, rumored to be Huawei chips, demonstrating China’s growing capability to develop and rely on domestic AI hardware.

LongCat 2.0 is a mixture-of-experts model, where only 48 billion of its 1.6 trillion parameters are activated during use, making it efficient for coding and long-context tasks. The model outperforms Google’s Gemini 3.1 Pro in agentic coding and reasoning benchmarks and is open-source, allowing integration with tools like Claude Code and OpenClaw. However, due to its size, running LongCat 2.0 locally requires enterprise-grade GPUs.

sections.heading: LiveEdit: Real-Time Video Editing
sections.body:
LiveEdit is an AI tool that enables real-time video editing, allowing users to make changes to videos as they play. The tool processes videos at nearly 13 frames per second and supports a wide range of edits, including replacing clothing, changing weather, removing objects, and altering styles. LiveEdit achieves this by adapting regular video models into a causal chunk-wise streaming setup, which processes frames in small chunks rather than requiring the entire video at once.

The tool is open-source, with a total size of 17 GB, making it suitable for mid to high-end GPUs. Training scripts are also provided, offering transparency and customization options for developers.

sections.heading: Brain to QWERTY: Turning Thoughts into Text
sections.body:
Meta’s Brain to QWERTY is an AI project that converts brain signals into typed text without requiring surgery. The system uses magnetoencephalography (MEG) to record brain activity and decodes it into sentences. Version 2 of the model was trained on 10 times more data than its predecessor, achieving 69% character accuracy and 78% word accuracy. While not perfect, this represents a significant step forward in non-invasive brain-computer interfaces.

The code for Brain to QWERTY is publicly available, allowing researchers and developers to experiment with the technology. This could have profound implications for accessibility and human-computer interaction.

sections.heading: Claude 3.5 Fable: Re-Release with Stricter Safeguards
sections.body:
Anthropic re-released Claude 3.5 Fable after a brief ban by the US government due to safety concerns. The new version includes improved safety classifiers that block specific jailbreak attempts in over 99% of cases, though this has led to more false positives. Users can only access Fable via a subscription plan until July 7th, after which it will transition to usage credits. Additionally, Fable 5’s weekly usage is limited to 50% of a user’s total allowance, and routine coding tasks now fall back to the less capable Opus 4.8 model.

Benchmarks show mixed results for the new Fable, with reduced performance in debugging and refactoring tasks compared to its predecessor. While it remains one of the most powerful models available, its usability is significantly impacted by these restrictions.

sections.heading: Google’s Efficient Models: Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
sections.body:
Google introduced two lightweight models focused on efficiency: Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash. Nano Banana 2 Lite is designed for high-throughput image generation, producing images in just 4 seconds at a cost of slightly over 3 cents per 1,000 images. While its quality is slightly lower than the full Nano Banana 2 model, it is significantly faster and cheaper.

Gemini Omni Flash is a video generator and editor that allows users to edit videos using natural language prompts. It supports tasks like adding, replacing, or editing objects and styles. Although its quality is lower than the full Omni model, it outperforms competitors like Alibaba’s Happy Horse and Kling in Google’s benchmarks. Both models are available for free on platforms like Google Flow, with generous daily limits.

sections.heading: Speeding Up Image Generation: RDM and Mr. Flow
sections.body:
RDM (Representation Distribution Matching) and Mr. Flow are two innovative methods for speeding up AI image generation. RDM generates images in a single step by matching the distribution of high-level visual features, rather than relying on the traditional multi-step diffusion process. While the quality is slightly lower than full diffusion models, it offers a significant speed advantage.

Mr. Flow speeds up image generation by first creating a low-resolution image, upscaling it, and then refining it with a single high-resolution step. This method can accelerate generation by up to 21 times for models like Z-Image Turbo, with minimal quality loss. Both methods are training-free and model-agnostic, making them versatile tools for developers.

faq.question: What is Muse VIT, and how does it work?
faq.answer: Muse VIT is an AI model designed to understand sheet music. It was trained on 9.7 million pages of sheet music by masking parts of the music and tasking the AI with reconstructing the missing sections. This process helps the model learn the structure, symbols, and notation of sheet music, making it more accurate than general vision models for this task.

faq.question: How did Meituan train LongCat 2.0 without NVIDIA GPUs?
faq.answer: Meituan trained LongCat 2.0 using AI ASIC superpods, which are specialized chips for AI training. These chips are rumored to be Huawei’s, demonstrating China’s ability to develop domestic alternatives to NVIDIA hardware for large-scale AI training.

faq.question: What are the limitations of the re-released Claude 3.5 Fable?
faq.answer: The re-released Claude 3.5 Fable has stricter safety measures, leading to more false positives and rejections of benign requests, including coding tasks. It also falls back to the less capable Opus 4.8 model for routine coding and debugging tasks, and users are limited to 50% of their weekly usage allowance for Fable.

faq.question: How does LiveEdit achieve real-time video editing?
faq.answer: LiveEdit processes videos in small chunks using a causal chunk-wise streaming setup, allowing it to edit videos in real-time at nearly 13 frames per second. This approach avoids the need to process the entire video at once, enabling seamless edits as the video plays.

faq.question: What is Brain to QWERTY, and how accurate is it?
faq.answer: Brain to QWERTY is a Meta project that converts brain signals into text using MEG (magnetoencephalography) without requiring surgery. Version 2 of the model achieves 69% character accuracy and 78% word accuracy, representing a significant advancement in non-invasive brain-computer interfaces.

seo_description: Explore the latest AI advancements, including Meituan’s LongCat 2.0 trained without NVIDIA GPUs, real-time video editing with LiveEdit, and Meta’s Brain to QWERTY.
